_description: Methodology template: Healthcare Access Gap Analysis. Pre-fills analysis section with evidence-based method stack for uninsured rate / preventive care gap work at the census-tract level.

client.notes: Ask about Medicaid expansion status for the study state(s) — it materially affects baseline uninsurance rates.

audience.role: Health planner, CHC director, or county health officer
audience.what_they_are_deciding: Where to site new FQHCs, target outreach, or make the case for Medicaid expansion.
audience.what_they_already_believe: Uninsurance is concentrated in rural areas and communities of color.
audience.political_or_institutional_context: Medicaid expansion politics may be sensitive. Frame as access gap, not as partisan argument.

engagement.hero_question: Which census tracts have the highest unmet need for primary care, and what demographic and structural factors explain that gap?
sensitive_findings_to_handle_carefully: FQHC site-level data may be politically sensitive if clients are competitors
Rural vs urban framing may alienate urban stakeholders

known_spatial_issues: HPSA designations lag real conditions by 1-3 years
FQHCs serve variable catchment areas — point-in-polygon overstates access
Rural tracts are large — centroid-based drive times understate travel burden

primary_sources.name: HRSA Health Center Finder (FQHCs)
primary_sources.notes: Download GeoJSON of all 330 health centers. Filter by state.

primary_sources.name: HRSA HPSA Designations
primary_sources.notes: Primary care HPSAs at tract or county level. Join on GEOID.

primary_sources.name: TIGER/Line Tracts
primary_sources.notes: Use vintage matching ACS data year

primary_sources.name: CDC PLACES (optional)
primary_sources.notes: Tract-level preventive care utilization rates. Useful as alternate dependent variable.

known_data_quality_issues: ACS uninsured estimates have high MOEs in low-population tracts
HPSA scores are ordinal, not continuous — treat carefully in regression
FQHCs serve sliding-scale populations regardless of insurance status
institutional_areas_to_flag: Prisons and group quarters inflate tract population without increasing outpatient demand
College tracts skew young-healthy — may show low uninsurance but low utilization

analysis_notes.choropleth: Use natural breaks (Jenks) with 5 classes. Highlight HPSA-designated tracts with hatching overlay.
analysis_notes.proportional_symbols: Use uninsured_count (ACS B27001). Scale circles by population. Overlay on choropleth of poverty_rate.
analysis_notes.service_areas: Run compute_service_areas.py from FQHC point locations. 10/20/30-minute drive bands. Dissolve by time band. Intersect with tract centroids to flag 'outside 30min coverage.'
analysis_notes.regression: Run OLS first, check LM diagnostics. Expect spatial lag or error dependence. If rho significant, interpret: spatial diffusion of uninsurance across tract boundaries.
analysis_notes.hotspots: Getis-Ord Gi* on uninsured_rate. Identify cold spots (high coverage) and hot spots (access deserts).

report.pyramid_lead: An estimated [X]% of residents in [Study Area] lack health insurance, concentrated in [N] census tracts where HPSA-designated primary care shortages overlap with high poverty rates.

scqa.situation: [Study Area] has [X] residents across [N] census tracts. Primary care access is the key health equity metric for the region.
scqa.complication: [X]% are uninsured and [N] tracts are designated Health Professional Shortage Areas, yet only [M] FQHCs serve the area.
scqa.question: Which tracts represent the highest-priority access gaps, and what structural factors drive them?
scqa.answer: Tracts with HPSA designation, high poverty, and low FQHC proximity account for [X]% of uninsured residents. Spatial lag regression confirms clustering — high-uninsurance tracts are surrounded by similar tracts.

_methodology_caveats: Medicaid expansion: If the state has expanded Medicaid, uninsurance rates reflect post-expansion baseline. Non-expansion states will show systematically higher rates — do not compare across expansion status without adjustment.

FQHC vs uninsurance distinction: FQHCs are designed to serve uninsured and underinsured patients. High FQHC count does not mean uninsurance is adequately addressed — it may reflect targeted siting in high-need areas. Interpret fqhc_count negative coefficient cautiously.
HPSA lag: HPSA designations are updated annually but reflect applications submitted 1-3 years prior. A tract may have unmet need without HPSA designation.
Drive-time assumptions: Service area isochrones assume average road speed and ignore appointment availability, language barriers, and transportation access beyond vehicle ownership.
ACS MOEs: In low-population tracts, ACS uninsured estimates may have MOEs exceeding the estimate itself. Flag tracts with MOE/estimate > 0.5 in technical appendix.
